Tafseer:
The way to the correct concept regarding the Creator of this universe:
The word 'Tabaraka' contains the meanings of both greatness and blessing, and at the same time, this form is also intensive. Therefore, its meaning is that highly great and blessed is the Being in whose hand of power lies the rein of this universe. 'And He is over all things competent' - and along with being great and blessed, He is also powerful over all things. No action, no matter how great and difficult, can be conceived as being outside the sphere of His possibility. (Footnote 1)
This states the condition of the observation that appears, or should appear, before a rational and thoughtful person when they reflect on the signs of this universe. The proof of this is coming next. Here, the conclusion of the thought is stated first so that this reality becomes clear to everyone: one who reflects on this universe can never conceive that its Creator is playing a game, or that He is careless and irresponsible—having created this world but having no interest in its good and evil—or that He is merely a prime mover from whom a single movement was initiated but who has nothing to do with the consequences of that movement, or that He is merely a silent cause of causes who has no relation with His effects other than being their cause.
Those who fell into such concepts regarding the Creator of this universe did so either because they did not want to conceive of Him correctly—lest this concept interfere with their pursuit of desires—or they wanted to do so but, instead of looking at the reflection of His attributes in the mirror of the vast and great universe He created, they tried to look through the tiny spectacles of their own invention. However, the only correct method was to observe the universe He created with complete sincerity and impartiality, and see the manifestation of His attributes within it. Had they done so, the reality would have become clear to them: its Creator is both highly great and highly blessed and wise, and at the same time, His power is limitless. He can do whatever He wills; no task is difficult or impossible for Him. From this concept, it is obvious that the root of all those false concepts in which polytheistic nations were involved is severed, and no room remains for those delusions in which philosophers and scientists became entangled.
_____
Footnote 1: The implications of the word 'Tabaraka' have been discussed under Surah Al-Furqan, verses 1, 10, and 61.

The inevitable consequences of the correct concept:
This states the same point in a different style, which further explains His power, wisdom, and blessing. It is stated: He is the One Who created death and life. No one else has any authority over either of them. Furthermore, death is mentioned before life, which is a proof that everything came into existence from the veil of non-existence into the world of existence solely by the power and blessing of Allah Almighty; if He had not willed, nothing could have come into existence.
Life after non-existence, and then death after life, is a testimony that the machinery of this world is not without purpose or objective—that it would just continue like this or end one day without any consequence. If that were the case, it would be a vain work, which is contrary to the status of a Wise, All-Powerful, and Blessed Being. Rather, it is a proof that whoever Allah Almighty blesses with life in this world, He does so to test them, to see who leads a life according to His pleasure and who follows his own desires. The requirement of this test is that He must inevitably bring a Day when He resurrects people, takes account of everyone's good and evil, and recompenses them according to their deeds.
In addition, He is 'Al-Aziz' (the All-Mighty); therefore, no one can save those who deserve punishment from His grasp. He is also 'Al-Ghafur' (the Forgiving); therefore, He will not deprive those who deserve His forgiveness, but they will become worthy of it without the need for anyone's efforts or intercession.

An invitation to observe the universe:
In the first verse, reference was made to Allah Almighty's power, greatness, blessing, and providence; in this verse, an invitation is given to observe these attributes: come, see the greatness and status of the Creator of this universe, His matchless craftsmanship, and His perfect art—how He created seven heavens, layer upon layer, and you cannot find any inconsistency, defect, or flaw in them despite your best efforts. Can anything be greater than the heavens? Yet, within this vast, endless, and boundless creation, the perfection of its Creator's art is such that even the most skilled expert cannot point a finger and say that there is any shortcoming in making some joint or structure level. The word 'tafawut' means difference, inconsistency, and unevenness. This same subject is described further by the word 'futur', which means defect and flaw. To express this same subject, the word 'furuj' is used in Surah Qaf, verse 6:
'Have they not looked at the heaven above them—how We structured it and adorned it and [how] it has no rifts?'
(Have they not looked at the heaven above them—how We structured it and adorned it and [how] it has no rifts?).

To establish the proof, the invitation is repeated: do not look just once, but turn your critical gaze again and again; your gaze will return to you fatigued and worn out, but you will not find any defect or flaw anywhere. The meaning is: for the God Whose matchless power and wisdom you see spread over your heads in this manner—such that you can neither encompass it nor find the slightest defect in it—what task can be difficult? Is raising you after death, accounting for your deeds, recompensing you, or destroying you in the twinkling of an eye through some earthly or heavenly calamity more difficult than creating these heavens?

Manifestations of mercy alongside power:
After referencing the seven heavens, attention is drawn specifically to the lowest heaven, the wonders of which can be observed relatively easily. It is stated: look at how We have adorned it with lamps! The meaning is: when you look at these lamps, you will realize that the Creator of this world is not only powerful, but also possessed of great mercy, having illuminated this ceiling with lamps whose beauty and benefit cannot be measured. Above, in 'you do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful' (ma tara fi khalqi-r-Rahman), reference is made to the attribute 'Ar-Rahman' (the Most Merciful). This points to this very aspect—showing that this world, by its very existence, not only testifies that it is created by a Being of great power, but also testifies that He is extremely merciful, generous, and caring toward His servants, having structured a ceiling adorned with such matchless lamps for them. In this Surah, reference to the attribute 'Ar-Rahman' will appear repeatedly. In every place, it is necessary to keep this specific aspect in view; otherwise, the true beauty of the text will remain hidden.
Reference to a secondary benefit of stars: Through the words 'and We have made them [as] projectiles for the devils' (wa ja'alnaha ruju-man li-sh-shayateen), reference is made to another secondary benefit of these stars—namely, that Allah Almighty also uses them as stations for stoning the devils. Here, the matter is stated briefly. Its details have appeared in other places in the Qur'an: Allah Almighty has established guard stations (buruj) within these stars, where His angels keep guard at all times. If the devils try to ascend to overhear the talk of the high assembly, the angels pelt them with shooting stars to drive them away. We have discussed the nature of these shooting stars in the commentary of Surah Ar-Rahman; refer to it for details. This secondary reference is intended to draw attention to the reality that Allah Almighty, after creating this world with such care, has not abandoned it for the devils to make it their playing field; rather, He has also arranged for its supervision, and when they exceed the limits, they are corrected. This proves that this creation is not a flock without a shepherd; rather, the One Who created it is supervising it with complete vigilance, and one day, all jinn and humans who cause corruption in it will face their end. The punishment of the Blaze is prepared for them: 'and We have prepared for them the punishment of the Blaze' (wa a'tadna lahum 'adhabas-sa'eer).

Denying the Resurrection is equivalent to denying God:
From the treatment of the devils mentioned above, the discourse transitions to the fate of those humans who disbelieve in their Lord. 'Disbelief in the Lord' here refers to denying the Resurrection and recompense. The reason for this—as we have explained in detail in various places in this book—is that denying the Resurrection nullifies all the fundamental attributes of Allah Almighty: His power, justice, mercy, and providence. Believing in God while denying these attributes is equivalent to not believing in Him at all. On this basis, the Qur'an repeatedly describes the polytheists as disbelievers, even though they did not deny the existence of God.
'And wretched is the destination' (wa bi'sa-l-maseer) - It is stated that this is a highly wretched place and return which they chose for themselves. Some aspects of its wretchedness are explained in the subsequent verses.

It is stated that when these deniers are cast into the Fire, they will hear it braying and roaring, just as a hungry lion roars upon seeing its prey, while it is boiling and raging—meaning its burning will be at its peak.

Depiction of the Fire's raging wrath: This describes the anger of Hell, showing as if it is about to burst with rage. The reason for its intense wrath is obviously that those who lived their lives in heedlessness of this terrifying Day did so by completely closing their eyes and ears. Otherwise, there was no lack of signs of the Resurrection and recompense in this world, nor was it ever devoid of warners. Therefore, those who possessed eyes and ears but did not benefit from them do not deserve any sympathy. Consequently, the keepers of Hell will rebuke them, saying: 'O unfortunate ones, did no warner come to you to warn you of this Day, so that you brought this misery upon yourselves?' At that moment, they will confess: 'There is no doubt that warners did come to us to warn us, but we rejected them, saying: "Allah has not revealed anything; you are merely claiming this to impose on us, asserting that God has sent you to warn us of this Day and to teach us the preparations needed for His pleasure."'

Depiction of the Fire's raging wrath:
This describes the anger of Hell, showing as if it is about to burst with rage. The reason for its intense wrath is obviously that those who lived their lives in heedlessness of this terrifying Day did so by completely closing their eyes and ears. Otherwise, there was no lack of signs of the Resurrection and recompense in this world, nor was it ever devoid of warners. Therefore, those who possessed eyes and ears but did not benefit from them do not deserve any sympathy. Consequently, the keepers of Hell will rebuke them, saying: 'O unfortunate ones, did no warner come to you to warn you of this Day, so that you brought this misery upon yourselves?' At that moment, they will confess: 'There is no doubt that warners did come to us to warn us, but we rejected them, saying: "Allah has not revealed anything; you are merely claiming this to impose on us, asserting that God has sent you to warn us of this Day and to teach us the preparations needed for His pleasure."'
'You are not but in great error' (in antum illa fi dalalin kabeer) - That is: 'Not only did we not accept anything they said and did not heed their warning regarding our deviation, but on the contrary, we accused them of being deviated, saying: "It is not us, but you who are in great error, telling us these warnings that we will be resurrected after death, our every word and action will be accounted for, and we and our ancestors will be cast into Hell."'
Although the word 'nadhir' (warner) mentioned above is singular, the pronoun 'antum' (you [plural]) is used here, which indicates that these people will also confess: 'This was the very response we gave to everyone who tried to warn us of this Day, whether he was a messenger of Allah or his companions.'

The confession of the people of Hell that they did not use their intellect: Not only this, but they will further confess that if they had listened to advice or used their intellect, the reality was so clear that they would have been on the path of guidance and worthy of Paradise, rather than being companions of the Blaze. However: 'We neither opened our ears to listen to the sincere advisors, nor did we use our own intellect; consequently, we reached this evil end.'

It is stated that in this manner, they will confess their sin, and by their own confession, they will prove themselves deserving of Hell; so away with these unfortunate ones who knowingly prepared their own path to Hell.

After describing the fate of the deniers of the Resurrection, this states the reward of those who feared their Lord in this world without seeing the Resurrection with their eyes. It is stated: for them indeed is a great mercy, forgiveness, and a magnificent reward.
The reward of those who use their intellect: Through the words 'who fear their Lord in the unseen' (yakhshawna rabbahum bil-ghayb), their status as people of intellect and insight is demonstrated—showing that they did not live their lives with closed eyes and ears, nor did they wait to believe only when everything became visible; rather, they reflected on the signs of this universe, listened attentively to those who warned them, and pondered over their words. Therefore, they deserve that Allah Almighty blesses them with His great favor. The primary test of human beings in this world is to use their intellect and insight to believe in the realities reported by Allah's messengers. Whoever passes this test is worthy of every reward from Allah Almighty, and whoever fails it is like an animal, or even worse than animals, no matter how great a philosopher or scientist he may be considered.

Warning to the deniers, comfort to the believers:
These verses can serve as both a warning and comfort. Here, they serve both purposes. For the deniers of the Resurrection mentioned above, there is a warning: do not remain in the misunderstanding that your Lord can remain unaware of your secret or open deeds. Whether you speak secretly or openly, He knows everything. He is fully aware of what is hidden in the breasts, so there is no distinction between secret and open before Him.
Following the deniers of the Resurrection, the state of those who fear God in the unseen was mentioned. For them, there is comfort here: whether your words and deeds are secret or open is entirely the same for God. The intimate conversations you have with your Lord in the privacy of the night are in His knowledge, what you do in the public of the day is before Him, and what is in your hearts is not hidden from Him. Since nothing is hidden from Him, be assured that not even a mustard seed's weight of your good deeds will go in vain; rather, you will receive a full reward for your every action.

'Does He not know, He Who created? And He is the Subtle, the Acquainted' (ala ya'lamu man khalaq wa huwa-l-lateefu-l-khabeer) - This is the proof showing why Allah Almighty is fully aware of your every secret and open deed. It is stated: since He is your Creator—and His being the Creator is something no one can deny—how can it be possible for the Creator to remain unaware of His creation? How can the One Who gave existence to all faculties and abilities, and by Whose command all machinery moves, remain unaware of any movement of His creation!
'And He is the Subtle, the Acquainted' - As clarified in various places, 'lateef' means highly observant, subtle, and perceptive. It is stated: the true subtle and aware Being is He alone. If anyone else knows an aspect of someone's life, his knowledge is partial and incomplete, whereas Allah Almighty brought everyone into existence and blesses them with life and sustenance; therefore, His knowledge encompasses the smallest of details.
It should be noted here that among the causes of shirk, a major one is the deviation that nations suffered regarding the knowledge of Allah Almighty. Unless the correct concept of God being All-Knowing and All-Acquainted is firmly established in the heart, neither can His fear be generated in a person, nor can they experience the true pleasure of trusting and relying on Him.

Reference to the signs of providence on earth:
Above, the arguments were drawn from the wonders of power and wisdom in the heaven; here, attention is drawn to the Resurrection through the signs of providence on earth. It is stated: He is the God Who made the earth subservient and compliant for you, so that you may walk in its high and low places, its valleys and mountains, benefit from the sustenance your Lord has spread in it, and remember the reality that one day everyone must be gathered to Him.
If you reflect on the words 'dhalul' (subservient/compliant) and 'manakib' (shoulders/slopes), you will realize that a metaphor is implied here. Specifically, the earth is compared to a compliant she-camel. Its passes, paths, valleys, and mountains are compared to the camel's 'manakib'—meaning its shoulders—and humans are assumed to be on the body of this she-camel like lice, whose entire sustenance is provided in its shoulders and slopes, where they move about and from where they obtain their food.
An essential requirement of providence: 'eat of His provision, and to Him is the resurrection' (kulu min rizqihi wa ilayhi-n-nushoor) - This is the sign that this arrangement of providence is conveying to human beings through its state: benefit from this sustenance and peace, and remember the reality that the God Who made this arrangement for you without any right on your part will not leave you like an unbridled camel without accountability; rather, you must die one day, be resurrected after death, and inevitably return to your Lord. This is because it is contrary to reason and nature that a human being should possess blessings and rights but remain free of accountability.

It is not fitting for a weak creature to be arrogant of its existence:
Referring to the weakness and insignificance of human beings mentioned in the preceding verse, this is a warning: the human who crawls like a louse in the vast corners of this earth should not be so arrogant of his power and resources that when warned of God's punishment, he mocks it, questioning where the punishment will come from and who will bring it. It is stated: are you completely secure and untroubled by the Great Being Who is in the heavens—that He could cause the earth to swallow you up while it is spinning and moving rapidly!
'Mawr' means moving rapidly, as is clear from: (The Day the heaven will sway with a rapid movement) (At-Tur, 52:9). People have translated it in various ways, but my mind constantly inclines to the view that here it refers to spinning out of control. One reason is that the root meaning of 'mawr' is rapid movement, and the second is that the earth was compared above to a compliant she-camel (naqatin dhalul). In this connection, this meaning seems highly appropriate here. The point is: it is solely out of God's mercy that He has made the earth subservient to you, serving as a compliant she-camel for your benefit; but if God relaxes its reins even a little, see how it will run out of control, beyond anyone's ability to manage.

Warning of a calamity from the atmosphere:
In the preceding verse, reference was made to a calamity appearing from beneath the feet; here, a warning is given of a calamity appearing from above the head: are you secure from your Lord, Who is in the heaven—that He could unleash upon you a wind carrying stones and pebbles?
'Hasib' refers to a storm wind carrying stones and pebbles. We have explained this in various places in this book. In the commentary of Surah Adh-Dhariyat, we also quoted the research of our teacher regarding it. It served as a major factor in the destruction of past nations, particularly the people of Lot, who were destroyed by this very calamity. Since the Quraysh frequently passed by the ruined settlements of the people of Lot, this example could be highly effective for them.
'Nadhir' is used here in the sense of a verbal noun (masdar), which is a recognized usage. That is: 'Today, My warning seems like a joke to you, but when it appears before you, you will realize how the thing you mocked becomes a reality and in what terrifying form it manifests.'

Instruction to learn a lesson from history:
This instructs the Quraysh to learn a lesson from history: if this punishment has not yet befallen them, they should not mock it because of this delay. It is not wise to believe only in what befalls one's own head; rather, they should learn a lesson from the accounts of other nations who were warned in a similar manner but mocked the warning, until the punishment they mocked eventually overtook them.
'Then how was My rejection' (fa-kayfa kana nakeer) - See how My rejection and wrath befell them! That is: with what loathing and displeasure I abandoned them to become the targets of My punishment, with no one to save them.

Everything is held in place solely by God's preservation:
That is, in this world, nothing—whether above or below—is self-operating or stands on its own; rather, Allah alone moves it and He alone holds it. The earth stands firm beneath our feet because God keeps it firm. If He did not hold it, as indicated above, it would slide away along with everyone. Similarly, the heaven stands firm over our heads not on its own, but because Allah Almighty holds it in place. If He were to release it, it might fall upon us. This reality is explained here through an example: do they not see the birds above them, spreading their wings and folding them? In both states, it is the Most Merciful God Who holds them in the air. If He did not hold them, they would fall immediately. The point is: project this to the stars and planets of this infinite space, its fixed and moving celestial bodies. If they remain in place, it is because God preserves them; otherwise, even one of them falling could devastate the entire earth.
'Indeed, He is of all things Seeing' (innahu bi-kulli shay'in baseer) - That is: it is solely out of the mercy of the Most Merciful God that He supervises and preserves everything; otherwise, if even a single screw of this universe were to loosen slightly, this entire world would be destroyed in the twinkling of an eye.

No army can defend against a divine strike:
That is, if you are demanding the punishment to be shown to you, what army do you possess that can help you against the Most Merciful God?
'The disbelievers are not but in delusion' (in-il-kafiruna illa fi ghurur) - This expresses regret over their wretchedness, showing that while their arrogance suggests they have built an invincible defense system that no power can breach, they are in deep delusion. If even a minor gust of divine punishment approaches, all their fortresses and barriers will be blown away like straw.

Context suggests that 'rizq' (provision) here refers to rain, which is the source of sustenance. This usage is common in the Qur'an. It is stated: suppose Allah Almighty withholds the rain that is the source of your sustenance, do you have any powerful entity who can reopen this closed door?
'But they have persisted in insolence and aversion' (bal lajju fi 'utuwwin wa nufur) - This expresses regret over their stubbornness: although they cannot dare to answer any of these questions in the affirmative, they persist in their rebelliousness and aversion to the truth. The point is: if they were willing to reflect, the matter could be explained to them; but there is no cure for stubbornness and defiance!

The primary cause of deviation:
This explains why the path of guidance is not opening for them, and why they continue to wander in deviation despite explanations.
It is stated: these people are like dogs, slaves to their desires. Just as a dog walks with its head down, sniffing the earth in the hope of finding something to eat, their guide is their desire instead of intellect, and they follow their desire with bowed heads and closed eyes. One who follows desire can never find the path of guidance. The path of guidance is found by the one who walks upright on a straight path, looking right, left, ahead, and behind. For this reason, Allah Almighty created human beings with an upright posture, not bowed toward the earth like animals; yet, many humans imitate the way of animals, losing the superior characteristic that is the true honor and distinction of human beings. Note that those who follow their desires are compared in various places in the Qur'an to animals, particularly dogs.

This expresses regret over their deprivation and ungratefulness. It is stated: Allah created you and equipped you with the superior faculties of hearing, sight, and intellect for a noble purpose, but you turned out to be ungrateful, imitating dogs and cattle instead of benefiting from these faculties, and choosing your desires as your guide instead of intellect and heart.

Reminding of the core reality in a new style:
This again reminds of the core reality: if you use your intellect, you should easily understand the obvious truth that the God Who sowed you in the earth and provides for you will not leave you to yourselves; rather, He will harvest His crop and gather it in His granary. Then, He will separate the grain from the chaff, gather the grain in the storehouse, and burn the chaff. When a farmer sows a crop in his field, provides it with fertilizer and water, and protects it from animals and birds, everyone knows without being told that he will harvest it one day, separating the grain from the chaff. Why do you not understand this same obvious truth regarding God? Did God create you in vain, and is this entire arrangement of your providence without purpose?
Note that the reality the Qur'an has described here in simple words is expressed in various styles in the ancient scriptures, particularly in the Gospel.
This verse has appeared in Surah Al-Mu'minun, and we explained it there as well; refer to verse 79 for details.

A foolish objection of the deniers:
That is, after hearing all of this, if they say anything, they ask: 'When will this promise be fulfilled?' Their only argument for denying the Resurrection is that if its occurrence is necessary, why does it not happen immediately, and why do those who warn of it not state its exact time? In their view, since the warners do not or cannot state its time, they are untruthful. It is commanded: answer these foolish people: 'The knowledge of its time is with Allah alone; I am only a clear warner.' No one knows when it will come, but it will surely come.
Since this objection is completely absurd, its response is not detailed here. However, in other places, it is explained: denying a reality merely because its exact time of occurrence cannot be stated is clear foolishness. We experience many events in our daily lives whose exact time of occurrence no one can state, yet everyone believes in their occurrence.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25 and 26 was combined in the original text.]

The transience of the deniers' arrogance:
That is, currently they are demanding to see this punishment with great arrogance, but this boasting will last only until it appears. When they see it approaching, their faces will turn pale and they will be struck dumb. At that moment, it will be said to them: 'This is the very thing you were demanding, so why are you losing your composure upon seeing it! This is your requested wish, so now taste it!'

An invitation to face reality instead of indulging in false comfort:
When the disbelievers were warned of the punishment, they would say to keep their people reassured: 'Do not be intimidated by this person. This threat of punishment is merely his eloquence and poetry. You will soon see that he too will perish, and all his words will blow away in the wind.' They threatened us with punishment while they themselves were waiting for some calamity to befall us and our companions. This theme is described in various places in the Qur'an; we present an example from Surah At-Tur, where He says:
(Or do they say, 'He is a poet for whom we await a calamity of time'? Say, 'Wait, for indeed I am with you among those who wait') (At-Tur, 52:30-31).
The meaning is: you are waiting for some calamity to befall us according to your assumption, and we are waiting for the punishment to befall you of which God has instructed us to warn you. We do not wish to argue with you regarding your assumption. Allah alone knows whether He will destroy us or show us mercy. We rely on Him, so we hope He will show us mercy. But suppose your assumption proves true and we perish, what comfort does that offer you? Who will save you from the wrath of God? The Resurrection is bound to happen; the proofs of its occurrence are solid. Recompense is certain, and there is no room to deny it or flee from it. The disbeliever and the believer, the righteous and the wicked, cannot be equal—this is an accepted reality. So, even if we assume we perish, how does that benefit you? You will still have to face the realities of which we are warning you! The point is: do not invite your ruin through such false comforts, but:
'Fear what is destined to come.'

That is, tell them: 'We do not make any claim regarding our future as to what will happen to us; its knowledge is with Allah alone. However, we know that Allah is the Most Merciful; we believe in Him and we rely on Him, so we hope He will show us mercy. If you wish to argue on this matter, wait; soon you will know who is in clear error—us or you?'

'Ma'un ma'een' refers to clear, pure, and fresh water.
A simple argument: water sinking deep into the earth can happen in two ways. One is that the water table drops so low that obtaining water from the earth becomes an extremely difficult task.
The second is that due to lack of rain, the water in canals, springs, and streams decreases and becomes muddy. Both meanings can be taken here. Because of the word 'ma'un ma'een' (clear water), my mind inclines to this second meaning.
Those who mock divine punishment have been warned above of several close realities; they should not regard it as impossible. God can seize you from wherever He wills. This final point of the series states: why look far? Look at your water: if it becomes muddy because its level has dropped, who can provide you with clear, fresh, and sweet water? So, what is the meaning of being secure and untroubled by the God in Whose hand lies every vein of your life!
